name: AWS Bedrock Agents API
what_it_does: AWS Bedrock Agents lets you build fully managed multi-step AI agents that can break down user requests, call APIs, query knowledge bases, and take actions — all with built-in memory, tracing, and guardrails.
use_cases: Building production agents that invoke AWS services and custom APIs via action groups
Connecting agents to enterprise knowledge bases (S3, SharePoint, Salesforce, Confluence)
Multi-step task decomposition with automatic retry and state management
Building agents with inline code execution (sandboxed Python)
Enterprise agents needing guardrails (harmful content filtering, PII redaction)
not_for: Rapid prototyping (setup is complex, not suitable for quick experiments)
Low-latency use cases (agent invocation adds 1-3s overhead per step)
Teams not already on AWS (vendor lock-in is significant)
Fine-grained billing control (pricing is opaque per-token)
best_when: You're building enterprise-grade agents on AWS and need managed orchestration, built-in knowledge bases, and AWS IAM-based security without managing your own agent loop.
avoid_when: You need fast iteration, multi-cloud portability, or are not already invested in the AWS ecosystem.

auth.notes: Standard AWS IAM authentication. Requires bedrock:InvokeAgent permission. Role-based access strongly recommended for agents — avoid long-lived access keys.

pricing.paid_tiers: Charged per 1K input/output tokens for the underlying model + orchestration overhead. Approximately 20-30% premium over direct model invocation for orchestration.
pricing.notes: No free tier for agents. Cost scales with number of orchestration steps. Can get expensive for complex multi-step workflows.

performance.rate_limits: 10 concurrent agent invocations per account by default; request increase for production

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: AWS SDK errors are verbose but not always actionable. Agent-specific errors (ResourceNotFoundException, ConflictException) are clear, but reasoning trace errors can be opaque.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Session IDs provide some idempotency but repeated invocations can produce different agent reasoning paths. No guaranteed idempotent execution.
known_agent_gotchas: Agent setup requires defining action group schemas in OpenAPI format — significant upfront work
Cold start latency: first invocation after idle period can take 5-10 seconds
Knowledge base vector sync is eventual — newly uploaded documents take minutes to be queryable
Inline agent sessions expire after 1 hour of inactivity — design for session resumption
Action group Lambda functions have 25MB payload limit — chunk large data
Guardrails add 200-500ms latency per step when enabled
Trace logging costs extra — essential for debugging but adds to bill
agent_readiness.security_notes: Strong IAM-based security model. KMS encryption at rest and in transit. VPC integration available for network isolation. Audit logging via CloudTrail.
